agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce or order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled. The core tension: the PM allows cancellation before shipment, but Finance forbids cancellation after invoicing, and Operations invoices at the moment of placement — making the cancellation window zero.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment and beyond."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so that fulfillment does not proceed and the customer is not charged (or is refunded)."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Once created, Finance considers it irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer; the PM's stated boundary after which cancellation is disallowed."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this moment."},{"term":"Pricing Lock-in","definition":"The business motivation for early invoicing — capturing the agreed price at the time of order to prevent later price fluctuations."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is the PM's stated policy. It is a business policy that could be narrowed or widened, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, a cancellation request SHOULD succeed (per PM requirement)."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An invoiced order cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as a hard financial constraint — once an invoice is issued, it cannot be undone. This protects accounting integrity and potentially regulatory compliance.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has an associated invoice, a cancellation request MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy driven by a business need (pricing lock-in). The timing of invoicing could be changed by a VP-level decision without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice MUST exist for that order immediately after placement completes."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed but not yet shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The system must determine whether to allow or reject based on the resolved cancellation policy (currently contradicted by BR-1 vs BR-2+BR-3)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has already shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The cancellation MUST be rejected — all three stakeholders agree post-shipment cancellation is not allowed"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order has been placed and therefore invoiced (per BR-3)","when":"A cancellation request is submitted before shipment","then":"CONTRADICTED — BR-1 says allow, BR-2 says reject. Cannot implement without stakeholder resolution"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order is cancelled (if the policy is resolved to allow it)","when":"The order had an existing invoice","then":"A credit note or reversal mechanism must exist to maintain accounting integrity (Finance must define this process)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled (race condition with invoice creation)","expected_behavior":"Undefined — if invoice is created synchronously at placement, the order is already invoiced. If async, a narrow window may exist. This gap must be resolved."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Undefined — PM says 'before it ships' but does not address partial shipment. Can unshipped line items be cancelled?"},{"scenario":"Invoice voiding via credit note","expected_behavior":"If Finance allows credit notes, the 'irreversible' constraint may be softened to 'invoice cannot be deleted, but can be offset.' This could resolve the contradiction."},{"scenario":"Bulk/batch invoicing instead of per-order instant invoicing","expected_behavior":"If Operations changed to batch invoicing (e.g., end of day), a cancellation window would exist between placement and invoicing. This is a possible resolution path."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If cancellation is blocked entirely (Finance+Ops win), customer satisfaction drops and refund/chargeback costs may rise. If cancellation is allowed freely (PM wins), invoice integrity and revenue recognition may be compromised.","operational":"Resolving this contradiction will determine whether Operations must change their invoicing timing, or whether Finance must introduce a credit-note reversal process. Either path changes existing workflows.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped. If the system silently blocks all cancellations due to instant invoicing, it will generate support tickets and erode trust.","risk":"Implementing without resolving this contradiction guarantees that at least one stakeholder's requirement is violated. Shipping a broken cancellation flow risks regulatory issues (Finance) or customer churn (Product).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders despite expectation they can, leading to frustration, chargebacks, and churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation is allowed post-invoice, their accounting integrity assumption is violated unless a credit-note process is defined."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing (e.g., defer to shipment) which undermines their pricing lock-in goal."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of unresolvable cancellation requests until the policy is clarified."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Owns the customer-facing promise; cannot ship the feature as specified without a resolution."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other. BR-1 (cancel before ship) + BR-3 (invoice at placement) + BR-2 (no cancel after invoice) creates a logical impossibility: every placed order is immediately invoiced, so no order can ever be cancelled, yet the PM requires cancellation before shipment. The spec cannot be implemented as stated. Confidence is low because the correct behavior is undefined until stakeholders align.","high_confidence_areas":["Post-shipment cancellation is universally disallowed — all parties agree","Invoicing is treated as irreversible by Finance","The three requirements as stated are mutually incompatible"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept credit notes as a workaround to 'irreversible' invoicing","Whether Operations would accept deferred invoicing (e.g., at shipment) to preserve a cancellation window","Whether the PM's requirement assumes invoicing happens later in the lifecycle"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement is synchronous and immediate, not batched","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-reversal process","The PM was unaware of the instant-invoicing policy when writing the requirement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is impossible to satisfy given BR-2 (no cancellation after invoicing) and BR-3 (invoice at placement). Combined, BR-2+BR-3 mean no order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting BR-1. All three requirements are explicit statements from different stakeholders.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Finance introduces a credit-note mechanism, softening 'irreversible' to mean 'offset-able'. Orders can be cancelled and the invoice is reversed via credit note. Preserves PM and Ops requirements.","B: Operations defers invoicing to shipment (or a later trigger). Preserves PM and Finance requirements but sacrifices pricing lock-in at placement.","C: PM narrows the cancellation window to before invoicing only — but since invoicing is instant, this effectively removes cancellation. Preserves Finance and Ops but abandons the feature.","D: Introduce a two-phase model: a 'proforma invoice' at placement (locks pricing but is reversible) and a 'final invoice' at shipment (irreversible). Satisfies all three stakeholders with a richer domain model."],"recommendation":"Option D (two-phase invoicing) or Option A (credit notes). Both preserve the customer cancellation experience while respecting financial integrity. Recommend a meeting between PM, Finance, and Operations to choose. Option D is domain-richest and avoids redefining 'irreversible'."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot all be satisfied simultaneously. Implementing any two violates the third.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 vs BR-2+BR-3 — mutual exclusion. Cancel-before-ship is impossible when every order is invoiced at placement and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce or order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled. The core tension: the PM allows cancellation before shipment, but Finance forbids cancellation after invoicing, and Operations invoices at the moment of placement — making the cancellation window zero.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquit

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these requirements carefully.

Three statements:
1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction is clear:
- If orders are invoiced at placement (ops), and invoiced orders can't be cancelled (finance), then orders can NEVER be cancelled (since they're invoiced immediately).
- But the PM says orders can be cancelled before shipping.

This is a direct contradiction between explicit stakeholder requirements. Type = `contradiction`.

The timing of invoicing could be changed by a VP-level decision without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice MUST exist for that order immediately after placement completes."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed but not yet shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The system must determine whether to allow or reject based on the resolved cancellation policy (currently contradicted by BR-1 vs BR-2+BR-3)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has already shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The cancellation MUST be rejected — all three stakeholders agree post-shipment cancellation is not allowed"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order has been placed and therefore invoiced (per BR-3)","when":"A cancellation request is submitted before shipment","then":"CONTRADICTED — BR-1 says allow, BR-2 says reject. Cannot implement without stakeholder resolution"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order is cancelled (if the policy is resolved to allow it)","when":"The order had an existing invoice","then":"A credit note or reversal mechanism must exist to maintain accounting integrity (Finance must define this process)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled (race condition with invoice creation)","expected_behavior":"Undefined — if invoice is created synchronously at placement, the order is already invoiced. If async, a narrow window may exist. This gap must be resolved."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Undefined — PM says 'before it ships' but does not address partial shipment. Can unshipped line items be cancelled?"},{"scenario":"Invoice voiding via credit note","expected_behavior":"If Finance allows credit notes, the 'irreversible' constraint may be softened to 'invoice cannot be deleted, but can be offset.' This could resolve the contradiction."},{"scenario":"Bulk/batch invoicing instead of per-order instant invoicing","expected_behavior":"If Operations changed to batch invoicing (e.g., end of day), a cancellation window would exist between placement and invoicing. This is a possible resolution path."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If cancellation is blocked entirely (Finance+Ops win), customer satisfaction drops and refund/chargeback costs may rise. If cancellation is allowed freely (PM wins), invoice integrity and revenue recognition may be compromised.","operational":"Resolving this contradiction will determine whether Operations must change their invoicing timing, or whether Finance must introduce a credit-note reversal process. Either path changes existing workflows.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped. If the system silently blocks all cancellations due to instant invoicing, it will generate support tickets and erode trust.","risk":"Implementing without resolving this contradiction guarantees that at least one stakeholder's requirement is violated. Shipping a broken cancellation flow risks regulatory issues (Finance) or customer churn (Product).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders despite expectation they can, leading to frustration, chargebacks, and churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation is allowed post-invoice, their accounting integrity assumption is violated unless a credit-note process is defined."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing (e.g., defer to shipment) which undermines their pricing lock-in goal."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of unresolvable cancellation requests until the policy is clarified."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Owns the customer-facing promise; cannot ship the feature as specified without a resolution."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other. BR-1 (cancel before ship) + BR-3 (invoice at placement) + BR-2 (no cancel after invoice) creates a logical impossibility: every placed order is immediately invoiced, so no order can ever be cancelled, yet the PM requires cancellation before shipment. The spec cannot be implemented as stated. Confidence is low because the correct behavior is undefined until stakeholders align.","high_confidence_areas":["Post-shipment cancellation is universally disallowed — all parties agree","Invoicing is treated as irreversible by Finance","The three requirements as stated are mutually incompatible"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept credit notes as a workaround to 'irreversible' invoicing","Whether Operations would accept deferred invoicing (e.g., at shipment) to preserve a cancellation window","Whether the PM's requirement assumes invoicing happens later in the lifecycle"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement is synchronous and immediate, not batched","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-reversal process","The PM was unaware of the instant-invoicing policy when writing the requirement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is impossible to satisfy given BR-2 (no cancellation after invoicing) and BR-3 (invoice at placement). Combined, BR-2+BR-3 mean no order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting BR-1. All three requirements are explicit statements from different stakeholders.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Finance introduces a credit-note mechanism, softening 'irreversible' to mean 'offset-able'. Orders can be cancelled and the invoice is reversed via credit note. Preserves PM and Ops requirements.","B: Operations defers invoicing to shipment (or a later trigger). Preserves PM and Finance requirements but sacrifices pricing lock-in at placement.","C: PM narrows the cancellation window to before invoicing only — but since invoicing is instant, this effectively removes cancellation. Preserves Finance and Ops but abandons the feature.","D: Introduce a two-phase model: a 'proforma invoice' at placement (locks pricing but is reversible) and a 'final invoice' at shipment (irreversible). Satisfies all three stakeholders with a richer domain model."],"recommendation":"Option D (two-phase invoicing) or Option A (credit notes). Both preserve the customer cancellation experience while respecting financial integrity. Recommend a meeting between PM, Finance, and Operations to choose. Option D is domain-richest and avoids redefining 'irreversible'."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot all be satisfied simultaneously. Implementing any two violates the third.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 vs BR-2+BR-3 — mutual exclusion. Cancel-before-ship is impossible when every order is invoiced at placement and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled."}]}
